question: How many independent steam generators (SGs) are there in NuScale?

nuscale_ch01_0058.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Electrically Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0058.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuScale NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design using an electrically heated 5x5 simulated fuel bundle with AREVA HTP™/HMP™ grid technology. The tests, performed at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics facility, aimed to gather CHF data, validate CHF correlations, determine mixing coefficients, and measure pressure drop characteristics under various hydraulic conditions to support the NuScale reactor safety analysis.

nuscale_ch01_0109.title: Figure 1.7-1a: Electrical Symbols
nuscale_ch01_0109.summary: This section provides a legend for electrical symbols used in NuScale’s single-line diagrams, detailing the meaning of each symbol and the associated information such as ratings, quantities, and types for transformers, breakers, batteries, switches, and other electrical components. It serves as a reference to interpret the electrical drawings and understand the specifications and functions of various devices represented in the diagrams.

nuscale_ch01_0451.title: BTP 10-2, Rev 4: Design Guidelines for Avoiding Water Hammers in Steam GeneratorsPSGD B.1 thru B.4Preheat Steam Generator Designs
nuscale_ch01_0451.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use top-feed, preheat, or once-through steam generator designs, so the regulatory guidelines for avoiding water hammers in these systems are not applicable. Instead, NuScale uses a different system (DHRS) that operates via passive natural circulation and inherently avoids the water hammer issues addressed by these guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0452.title: BTP 10-2, Rev 4: Design Guidelines for Avoiding Water Hammers in Steam GeneratorsOTSGD B.1 Once-Through Steam Generator Designs - Auxiliary Feedwater Supply
nuscale_ch01_0452.summary: This section explains that the NuScale plant design does not use the steam generator configurations (top-feed, preheat, or once-through with auxiliary feedwater) addressed by BTP 10-2, Rev 4 guidelines for avoiding water hammers. Instead, NuScale uses a passive Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) that fulfills a similar safety function without the need for auxiliary feedwater pumps, and its design inherently avoids water hammer concerns specified in the guidelines.

nuscale_ch01_0108.title: 8.37 ALARA Levels for Effluents from Materials Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0108.summary: This section provides a legend for electrical symbols used in NuScale's Final Safety Analysis Report, specifically for single-line diagrams. It details the notation and labeling conventions for various electrical components such as transformers, circuit breakers, batteries, switches, and other devices, ensuring clarity and consistency in interpreting the facility's electrical schematics.

nuscale_ch01_0059.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Fluid-Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0059.summary: The electrically-heated steam generator test at the SIET facility in Italy used instrumented tubes with controlled boundary conditions to simulate different heat flux regions, enabling precise measurement and analysis of secondary-side performance. The testing included various methods (adiabatic, diabatic, transient, and density wave oscillation) to gather data for evaluating pressure fluctuations, informing design parameters, and benchmarking NuScale’s thermal-hydraulic models, with oversight from the NRC.

nuscale_ch01_0018.title: Principal Design Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0018.summary: The section outlines the principal design criteria for the NuScale plant, focusing on the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) and the overall facility process. It describes the reactor core's heat transfer process, the natural circulation of coolant, and the generation of superheated steam to drive a turbine and produce electricity, with heat ultimately removed via a shared circulating water loop and condenser.

nuscale_ch01_0028.title: Instrumentation, Controls, and Electrical Systems
nuscale_ch01_0028.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design uses passive components—three vent valves and two recirculation valves—to remove decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident, operating without active power or operator intervention. The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) also functions passively, supplying clean, compressed air to protect operators from hazardous releases, with enough capacity for at least 72 hours. Fission product removal relies on the containment vessel (CNV) and isolation system, with passive processes inherent to the module’s design.

nuscale_ch01_0057.title: Critical Heat Flux Testing - NuFuel HTP2™ Fuel Design
nuscale_ch01_0057.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. The testing aimed to obtain CHF data to develop and validate NuScale's CHF correlations, as well as to gather information on subchannel temperatures, mixing coefficients, and pressure drop characteristics under various hydraulic conditions. The tests were performed using a 5x5 electrically-heated simulated fuel bundle at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics facility in Germany.

nuscale_ch01_0024.title: Emergency Core Cooling System
nuscale_ch01_0024.summary: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) in the NuScale design is a fully passive system that removes decay heat during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) using three independent reactor vent valves and two recirculation valves, which operate without active power. The system vents steam into containment for condensation and recirculates water to the core, with continued cooling achieved by heat conduction to the reactor pool.

edges.description: Section A discusses testing and validation of fuel and thermal-hydraulic performance for NuScale's steam generators, while Section B outlines regulatory guidelines and clarifies that certain large PWR requirements do not apply to NuScale's design, both referencing the steam generator design but not directly addressing the number of independent SGs.

Section A discusses testing and validation of the NuScale fuel and steam generator thermal-hydraulic performance, while Section B outlines regulatory guidelines for steam generator auxiliary feedwater systems, with both sections referencing the NuScale steam generator design but not directly addressing the number of independent steam generators.
Section B provides specific details about the test setup (three isolated tubes) referenced in Section A's general description of the steam generator thermal-hydraulic performance testing.

Section A and Section B both reference BTP 10-2, Rev 4 and discuss the applicability of design guidelines for steam generators and auxiliary feedwater systems to the NuScale design, specifically noting that certain guidelines for large PWRs do not apply to NuScale's DHRS.

Section B describes thermal-hydraulic performance testing of three isolated steam generator tubes, which provides experimental data relevant to determining the number of independent steam generators referenced in the analysis question, while Section A discusses regulatory guidelines and conformance but does not specify the number of SGs.

Section B provides experimental details about the number of isolated tubes tested (three), which may be interpreted as evidence supporting the number of independent steam generator modules referenced in Section A's broader design context.
